title: Incoterms 2020 — Who Owns the Risk, Where
summary: Mapping the 11 Incoterms rules against risk transfer points, cost boundaries, and which mode of transport each one fits.
content:
## Why Incoterms matter

Incoterms 2020 define exactly where **risk** and **cost** pass from seller to buyer. Getting this wrong on an LC or PO is an expensive mistake.

### Grouped by mode

- **Any mode of transport:** EXW, FCA, CPT, CIP, DAP, DPU, DDP
- **Sea/inland waterway only:** FAS, FOB, CFR, CIF

### Key distinctions I keep re-checking

1. **FOB vs CIF** — FOB risk passes once goods are on board; CIF the seller also pays freight + insurance to destination, but risk still passes on board at origin.
2. **DDP** — maximum seller obligation, seller even clears import duty.
3. **EXW** — minimum seller obligation, buyer arranges everything from the seller's door.

### Applied note

For RMG export contexts (Bangladesh), FOB and CIF dominate. Buyers often push for FOB to control their own freight forwarders.

title: Letter of Credit Mechanics — UCP 600 & SWIFT MT700
summary: Breaking down how an LC actually moves money and documents between issuing bank, advising bank, and beneficiary — with the GSK/Symbiotec case as reference.
content:
## The core flow

Applicant (buyer) → Issuing Bank → Advising/Confirming Bank → Beneficiary (seller)

An LC is a **bank's payment guarantee**, conditional on the beneficiary presenting exactly the documents specified — nothing more, nothing less. Discrepancies = bank can refuse payment.

### SWIFT MT700

The standard message format issuing banks use to open an LC. Fields worth remembering:

- **Field 45A** — description of goods
- **Field 46A** — documents required
- **Field 47A** — additional conditions

### GSK / Symbiotec case takeaway

Pharma imports carry extra document scrutiny (batch certificates, regulatory approvals) layered on top of standard LC docs — a reminder that LC document lists are industry-specific, not generic.

title: Kraljic Matrix — Segmenting the Supplier Base
summary: Using profit impact vs supply risk to classify purchases into Strategic, Leverage, Bottleneck, and Non-critical — and what sourcing strategy fits each quadrant.
content:
## The two axes

- **X-axis:** Supply risk (availability, number of suppliers, substitutability)
- **Y-axis:** Profit impact (volume purchased, % of total cost)

### Four quadrants

| Quadrant | Profit Impact | Supply Risk | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic | High | High | Partnership, joint planning |
| Leverage | High | Low | Competitive bidding, negotiate hard |
| Bottleneck | Low | High | Secure supply, find alternates |
| Non-critical | Low | Low | Simplify/automate ordering |

### Why it matters for BD sourcing

Most RMG raw material sourcing (yarn, dyes for niche colors) sits in **Bottleneck** — few suppliers, but low overall spend %. That changes the negotiation posture completely versus a Leverage item.

title: Supplier Performance Evaluation — Multiplicative Scoring Model
summary: Worked example scoring ABC Limited at 49.22% using a weighted multiplicative model across quality, delivery, cost, and service.
content:
## The model

Unlike additive scoring (weights just add up), a **multiplicative model** penalizes suppliers harder when they fail badly on even one criterion — a zero on any factor can crater the total score, which mirrors real risk exposure better.

### ABC Limited exercise result

Final score: **49.22%**

Weak spots were concentrated in on-time delivery consistency, which dragged the composite score down disproportionately versus its quality score — exactly the behavior a multiplicative model is designed to expose.

### Takeaway

When picking a scoring model for a real supplier scorecard, additive is easier to explain to stakeholders, but multiplicative gives a more honest signal on suppliers with one critical failure point.

title: MRP — Working Through a Material Requirements Plan
summary: Gross-to-net requirements, lot sizing, and lead time offsetting in an MRP exercise.
content:
## MRP logic in one line

Gross Requirements − Available Inventory − Scheduled Receipts = **Net Requirements**, then offset backward by lead time to get the **Planned Order Release** date.

### Steps I followed

1. Explode the BOM (Bill of Materials) for each finished good
2. Net against on-hand inventory + open POs
3. Apply lot-sizing rule (lot-for-lot vs fixed order quantity)
4. Offset by lead time to know *when* to release the order

### Where this connects to RMG

Fabric and trims ordering timelines are exactly this problem — miss the lead-time offset and the sewing line stops, which is the operational root of a lot of RMG delay penalties.

title: Christopher & Peck — Supply Chain Resilience Framework
summary: Core framework for my PGDSCM term paper on RMG sector disruption and resilience — four resilience-building strategies from Christopher & Peck.
content:
## Four resilience strategies (Christopher & Peck)

1. **Supply chain re-engineering** — build in strategic buffers, not just lean everything
2. **Collaboration** — information sharing across tiers, not just transactional buying
3. **Agility** — ability to respond quickly to demand/supply shifts
4. **Risk management culture** — treat SC risk as a continuous process, not a one-time audit

### Applying to Bangladesh RMG / COVID-19

This is the backbone of my term paper: *Supply Chain Disruption and Resilience Strategies in Bangladesh's RMG Sector — Lessons from COVID-19*. COVID exposed over-reliance on single-country sourcing (mainly China for fabric/trims) and thin buffer stock across most factories.

### Status

Full draft in progress — this entry will get updated as the paper develops further, this is also becoming a LinkedIn article for the SCM job search.

title: Cash Conversion Cycle & DuPont — Reading SCM Through Finance
summary: Why SCM decisions show up directly in CCC, DuPont ROE breakdown, and Free Cash Flow — the financial language procurement/logistics people need to speak.
content:
## Cash Conversion Cycle

**CCC = Days Inventory Outstanding + Days Sales Outstanding − Days Payable Outstanding**

Every inventory or supplier-payment-term decision I make as an SCM professional directly moves this number — it's the clearest bridge between operations and the CFO's priorities.

## DuPont breakdown

**ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Financial Leverage**

Inventory efficiency (a core SCM lever) sits inside Asset Turnover — meaning a warehouse/inventory improvement is not just an ops win, it is a shareholder-return story.

### Why I'm logging this

Going from BPO/QA into SCM, this is the vocabulary that turns "I reduced stockouts" into a sentence a hiring manager or CFO actually cares about.

title: Costing & Drop-or-Keep Decisions
summary: Coursework exercise on relevant costing for a drop-or-keep product line decision — separating sunk cost from relevant cost.
content:
## The core rule

Only **relevant costs** (future, differential, avoidable) belong in a drop-or-keep decision. Sunk costs and unavoidable allocated overhead are noise.

### Common trap

A product line can look unprofitable on a full-absorption income statement purely because of allocated fixed overhead — but dropping it may not actually save that overhead, meaning the "loss" is partly an accounting artifact, not a cash reality.

### Method

1. Identify avoidable fixed costs if the line is dropped
2. Compare contribution margin lost vs costs avoided
3. Check for opportunity cost of the freed capacity
